title: A Personal and Sovereign God
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
series: Lessons from the School of Prayer
source_files: Lessons from the School of Prayer 11 - A Personal and Sovereign God - Eph. 1_1-11 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Two Operating Truths: Sovereignty and Responsibility
subpoints: God is absolutely sovereign, but sovereignty never reduces human responsibility in Scripture
Human beings are accountable creatures — our choices have moral significance
Human responsibility never diminishes God’s sovereignty or makes God contingent on us
A day without prayer denies the sovereignty of God and glorifies human self-sufficiency

outline.title: Why Pray If God Has Predestined Everything?
subpoints: The question is sincere and common — not to be dismissed as rhetorical
Prayer is a means God has appointed, just as preaching is a means he has appointed
An unbeliever is dead, enslaved, blinded, hardened, and hostile to God (Eph. 2:1; Rom. 8:7)
Only a sovereign God can change such a person — this is why we must pray for them

outline.title: Prayer for the Lost Only Makes Sense Under Sovereignty
subpoints: If the lost person has ultimate self-determination, what are you asking God to do for him?
To pray for conversion is to ask God to overcome the person’s resistance — a sovereign act
The doctrine of election does not hinder prayer for the lost; it is its only foundation
God opened Lydia’s heart so that she paid attention — we pray for that same opening

outline.title: Prayer Functions Like Preaching: Ordained Means to Ordained Ends
subpoints: If God is sovereign, why preach? How shall they hear without a preacher? (Rom. 10:14)
God ordained not only the end (who is saved) but also the means (preaching and prayer)
Paul and Jeremiah were set apart for their ministry before they were born
We pray; God uses those prayers to bring his will to pass — this is why prayer works

outline.title: Conclusion: Sovereignty Is Prayer’s Best Friend
subpoints: Lack of prayer demonstrates a practical belief that we are self-sufficient and God is not God
Reformed believers in history — Carey, Judson, Paton — were driven to missions by election
We do not try to calculate God’s decrees for tomorrow; we just pray and trust
Even our prayers are a gift — the Spirit works within us to produce the prayer God will answer

summary:
This lesson opens the final theological section of the prayer series, addressing the relationship between the sovereignty of God and prayer. The teacher notes that near-universal confusion surrounds this topic and proposes to spend two sessions working through it. Two foundational truths run throughout: God is absolutely sovereign, but sovereignty never cancels human responsibility; and human beings are responsible, accountable creatures, but their responsibility never diminishes God’s sovereignty.

The practical implication is stated boldly at the outset: a day without prayer is a day that denies the sovereignty of God and glorifies human self-sufficiency. If God is sovereign and prayer is unnecessary, the logical conclusion is self-sufficiency. The New Testament alone contains roughly 155 verses on prayer, and the most prominent pray-er in the Gospels is Jesus himself — who also happens to be the sovereign God. Sovereignty and prayer can only function together.

The teacher then turns to the most common version of the objection: if God has predestined who will be saved, why pray for anyone’s conversion? He answers by examining the biblical description of the unregenerate person — dead in sin, enslaved, blinded by Satan, hardened against God, and in active rebellion. Given this picture, he argues, what exactly would someone who believes in ultimate human self-determination be asking God to do for such a person? The only coherent prayer for an unconverted person is a prayer asking God to overcome that person’s resistance — which is by definition a sovereign act. The doctrine of election is not an obstacle to prayer for the lost but its only solid foundation.

Finally, the teacher draws an analogy between prayer and preaching: just as God has ordained preaching as the means by which the elect come to faith, so he has ordained prayer as a means by which his will comes to pass. God ordained both the ends and the means. The sovereign God of election is prayer’s best friend: the more firmly we believe that only God can save, the more urgently we are driven to pray that he will do exactly that.
transcript:
## Introduction

We are nearing the end of our series on the prayers of Paul and the epistles, and these last two lessons tonight and next week, Lord willing, are not going to be based necessarily only on Pauline prayers. Because we’re going to spend a couple of nights talking about the theology of prayer, especially how the sovereignty of God and prayer work together. That’s the goal for tonight and next week, and then we will be done with this series on prayer for Wednesday night.

I think that, second only to the emotions evoked by the idea that God has decided to save some and not others, is the confusion generated by faulty efforts to understand the sovereignty of God and prayer. Why is it that we pray, if God is already in control of everything? Why is it that we pray if God already predestined everything that’s supposed to happen? That doesn’t make sense. So I want to take the time tonight to talk about that, because it’s such a serious subject. And then next week, Lord willing, we’re going to interact with some faulty beliefs concerning prayer and the sovereignty of God, and talk about the efficacy of prayer in light of the sovereignty of God.

## Two Operating Truths

As we do that tonight and next week, there will be two truths that will be running in the background throughout the lesson. We’re not going to necessarily explicitly address them each time, but they’re going to be the operating system. The first one is that God is absolutely sovereign, but his sovereignty never functions in Scripture to reduce human responsibility. So God is absolutely sovereign, but the Bible never teaches that because God is sovereign we are less responsible for our actions.

The second truth is related to it: human beings are responsible creatures — accountable creatures. We choose, we believe, we disobey, we respond, and there is moral significance to all our choices. But human responsibility never functions in the Scriptures to diminish God’s sovereignty or to make God absolutely contingent. God’s actions are never contingent on our actions. God is not waiting to see what we’re going to do in order to figure out what he’s going to do next. At the same time, we are responsible for everything that we do.

And I wanted to first look at the seriousness of prayer in the context of the sovereignty of God. A day without prayer is a day that totally denies the sovereignty of God and glorifies the will and the self-sufficiency of men. And any explanation of the sovereignty of God that does not include a loud call for prayer and a robust understanding of the need of prayer is not according to the Scriptures. So any idea that we might have of the sovereignty of God that does not call and propel and exalt prayer is not a proper understanding of the sovereignty of God according to the Scriptures.

Just the sheer volume of the references to prayer in the New Testament points out to us the importance of it and the necessity of it. In the New Testament, there are roughly 155 verses worth of teaching on prayer. That’s six verses longer than the whole book of Galatians. So there’s a lot on prayer in the New Testament. The majority of the references to prayer in the New Testament are either in the practice or the teaching of Jesus Christ, which just happens to also be the sovereign God. So you can see that the sovereignty of God and prayer only work together.

## Why Pray If God Has Predestined Everything?

So a question that a lot of people have is this: if you believe God works all things according to the counsel of his will, and that his knowledge of all things past, present, and future is infallible and cannot change, then what is the point of praying that anything happen? If the first part is true about God, what is the point of prayer?

[Discussion with congregation about various responses: community with God, obedience, prayer changes us, prayer is a means God uses.]

All right, I’m going to walk you through some things here. It is a fact that not believing that God works all things according to the counsel of his will is not a choice that we have. To think that God operates according to something else than his own will is to go against the Scriptures. Just an example: Ephesians 1:11 — in him we have obtained an inheritance, having been predestined according to the purpose of him who works all things according to the counsel of his will. So what is included in all things? All things. And he works all things according to the counsel of his will.

Usually this question is asked in relation to human decisions. If God has predestined some people to be his sons and chosen them before the foundation of the world, as Ephesians 1:4-5 says, then what’s the point of praying for anyone’s conversion? That’s the best case we’re going to deal with, because that’s one thing that we all do — pray for people’s salvation.

## Prayer for the Lost Only Makes Sense Under Sovereignty

The implicit argument of the question is that if prayer is to be possible, all men must have the power of self-determination. People think: in order for prayer to work, we have to have the power to determine what happens to us. Because otherwise, if God has already appointed everything, what’s the point?

So let’s take a moment to examine this argument by reflecting on one example — praying for the salvation of unbelievers. Why pray for anyone’s conversion if God has chosen before the foundation of the world who will be his children?

What does the Bible say about a person who is unregenerate? The Bible says that he or she is dead in trespasses and sins — Ephesians 2:1. It says that he or she is enslaved to sin — Romans 6 and John 8. According to Paul, the God of this world has blinded the mind of the unbeliever that he might not see the light of the gospel of the glory of Christ — 2 Corinthians 4:4. His heart is hardened against God — Ephesians 4:18. So that he is hostile to God and in rebellion against God — Romans 8:7. That’s the person who is unregenerate.

So let me ask you this: if that’s what the Bible teaches concerning the unregenerate person, and if you insist that this man must have the power of ultimate self-determination, what is the point of praying for him? If he or she is the one who is in charge of what happens to them, what’s the point of asking God to do anything? If this is a true picture of the unconverted — dead, enslaved, blinded, hardened, hostile — then we can’t ask God to overcome the man’s rebellion, for rebellion is precisely what the man is now choosing.

So if we pray for God to save him, what we would be praying is that God would overcome his choice, which is to be in rebellion against God, and take away his power of self-determination. You can see that prayer for the salvation of the unconverted only works if God is sovereign. If that’s not the case, how can God save this man unless he acts so as to change the man’s heart from hostility to trust? And he can only do that if he is the one who determines things, not the person.

So will you pray that God enlightens this man’s mind so that he truly sees the beauty of Christ and believes? If you pray this, you are in effect asking God to no longer leave the determination of the man’s will in his own power. You are asking God to do something within this man’s mind or heart so that he will surely see and believe. That is, you are conceding that the ultimate determination of the man’s decision to trust Christ is God’s, not merely his.

What I’m saying is that it is not the doctrine of God’s sovereignty that prevents prayer for the conversion of sinners. It is the doctrine that believes that each person can choose for himself whatever he wants. Because if that’s true, then God can’t do anything for that person. But the doctrine of the sovereignty of God actually drives us to pray for the unconverted because we believe that only God can change the unconverted.

It’s interesting that in history, the biggest push to missions has been in Reformed churches that believe that God is sovereign. William Carey, Adoniram Judson, John Paton — these are names in the hall of fame of missionary work in the golden age of missions, and every single one of them was a firm believer in the sovereignty of God in salvation. And that’s what drove them to missions — the belief, the truth that God is sovereign, that God had appointed people from every tribe, from every people’s group, from every tongue, from every nation to come to him.

## Prayer Functions Like Preaching: Ordained Means

So what is prayer? Prayer is a request — requesting that God would do something. Even when we pray, hallowed be your name, we are requesting something that God will do. So prayer, by its very nature, in essence, is inherently a request that God would act. But the only thing that God can do to save a lost sinner is to overcome his resistance to himself. If we insist that the lost person remains a self-determining person, then we are insisting that he remain without Christ. Because Jesus says, no one can come to me unless it is granted to him by the Father.

Now, someone might say, okay, granted that a person’s conversion is ultimately determined by God, I still don’t see the point of your prayer. If God chose before the foundation of the world who would be converted, what function does your prayer have? Well, my answer is that prayer has a function like that of preaching. Does this argument also apply to preaching? If God is sovereign, why preach? How shall the lost believe in whom they have not heard? And how shall they hear without a preacher? And how shall they preach unless they are sent — as Paul says, Romans 10:14. Belief in Christ is a gift from God. But God has ordained that the means by which man believes in Jesus is through the preaching of other people. It is simply naive to say that if no one spreads the gospel, all those predestined to be children of God would be converted anyway. That won’t happen. Unless the gospel is proclaimed, people will not be saved. That’s the means that God appointed for it to happen.

The reason it’s naive is because it overlooks the fact that the preaching of the gospel is just as predestined as the believing of the gospel. Paul was set apart for his preaching ministry before he was born — he says that in Galatians 1:15, as was Jeremiah, Jeremiah 1:5. Therefore, to ask ‘if we don’t evangelize, will the predestined be saved?’ just doesn’t make sense. God knows those who are his and he will raise up messengers to win them.

John Piper said something very helpful: you will certainly carry out God’s purpose no matter what. However, you will certainly carry out God’s purpose — but it makes a difference to you whether you serve like Judas or like John. Either way, you’re carrying out God’s purpose.

So prayer is like preaching in that it is a human act that God has ordained and which he establishes because it reflects the dependence of his creatures upon him. He has promised to respond to prayer, and his response is just as contingent upon our prayer as our prayer is in accordance with his will. He will answer our prayer because he has appointed our prayers.

The bottom line is this: God ordained the end and also the means by which the end will be accomplished. He has ordained the answers to all the prayers he is going to answer. But he also has ordained the prayers as the means by which he will bring the answers. This ends-and-means dynamic is clearly shown in Acts 2:23 and Acts 4:27-28.

## Conclusion: Sovereignty Is Prayer’s Best Friend

So we don’t know if I’m ordained to pray this prayer or not. There is a very important theological expression to deal with that. Be sure to write it down. This is it: who cares? Just pray. Election and predestination are hindsight doctrines. We don’t try to figure out what God planned for tomorrow. We just know that yesterday was exactly how God planned. So we pray. We pray and God uses those prayers to bring to pass his will. And that’s why prayer works — because our God is sovereign and appointed it as a means to bring all the things he’s planned to pass.

The absolute sovereignty of God is prayer’s best friend. If we believe in that, then we pray. Our lack of prayer is a demonstration that we don’t believe that God is in control of all things. It’s a demonstration that we believe the contrary — that we are self-sufficient and that God is not God at all. So God’s sovereignty should drive us to prayer.

[Discussion with congregation about how prayer gives us knowledge of God through seeing how he answers; and how our gratitude should drive us to spend much time in prayer.]

Let’s pray then. Father, we thank you that you’re a good God who is in control of all things. A God who tells us that we have to come with confidence before your throne of grace to find the grace that we need for every time of need. We pray that you dismiss us with your blessings and prepare us for your day. We pray this in Jesus’ name, amen.
